Strengthening-softening transition and maximum strength in
Schwarz nanocrystals
Hanzheng Xing a , Jiaxi Jiang a , Yujia Wang a,b , Yongpan Zeng a , Xiaoyan Li a,
a Center for Advanced Mechanics and Materials, Applied Mechanics Laboratory, Department of Engineering Mechanics, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China
b Institute of High Performance Computing (IHPC), Agency for Science, Technology and Research (A*STAR), Singapore, 138632, Singapore
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Schwarz nanocrystal
Curved grain boundary
Atomistic simulation
Grain size effect
Maximum strength
A B S T R A C T
Recently, a Schwarz crystal structure with curved grain boundaries (GBs) constrained by twin-boundary (TB)
networks was discovered in nanocrystalline Cu through experiments and atomistic simulations. Nanocrystalline
Cu with nanosized Schwarz crystals exhibited high strength and excellent thermal stability. However, the grain-
size effect and associated deformation mechanisms of Schwarz nanocrystals remain unknown. Here, we per-
formed large-scale atomistic simulations to investigate the deformation behaviors and grain-size effect of nano-
crystalline Cu with Schwarz crystals. Our simulations showed that similar to regular nanocrystals, Schwarz
nanocrystals exhibit a strengthening-softening transition with decreasing grain size. The critical grain size in
Schwarz nanocrystals is smaller than that in regular nanocrystals, leading to a maximum strength higher than that
of regular nanocrystals. Our simulations revealed that the softening in Schwarz nanocrystals mainly originates
from TB migration (or detwinning) and annihilation of GBs, rather than GB-mediated processes (including GB
migration, sliding and diffusion) dominating the softening in regular nanocrystals. Quantitative analyses of
simulation data further showed that compared with those in regular nanocrystals, the GB-mediated processes in
Schwarz nanocrystals are suppressed, which is related to the low volume fraction of amorphous-like GBs and
constraints of TB networks. The smaller critical grain size arises from the suppression of GB-mediated processes.
1. Introduction
The mechanical properties of nanocrystalline metals are mainly
determined by their microstructures and mean grain size [1,2]. As the–
grain size decreases, the strength of nanocrystalline metals first increases–
and then decreases gradually, exhibiting a strengthening-softening
transition [2 4]. Thus, there exists a critical grain size–
d _ { \mathrm { c } }
correspond-–
ing to a maximum strength [2 4]. When the grain size is larger than
d _ { \mathrm { c } } ,
the grain boundaries (GBs) block the dislocation motion, leading to
strengthening described by the well-known Hall Petch effect [1 4].– –
When the grain size is smaller than
d _ { \mathrm { c } } ,
, GB-mediated processes (including
GB migration, sliding and diffusion) dominate plastic deformation,
resulting in softening (referred to as the inverse Hall Petch effect) [1 4].
Recent study showed that such softening can also be interpreted by the
intergranular damage due to the dominance of grain boundary diffusion
[5]. Nanocrystalline metals exhibit high strength, but their ductility is
sacrificed since dislocation activities are limited in nanosized grains [2,
6]. To improve the ductility of nanocrystalline metals, a high density of
nanoscale twins bounded by twin boundaries (TBs) has been introduced
into micron- or submicron-sized grains to synthesize nanotwinned metals
[7 11]. TBs not only block the dislocation motion but also store certain
mobile dislocations [8,11 14]. Consequently, nanotwinned metals
exhibit a combination of high strength, good ductility [8,10,11] and
remarkable fatigue resistance [15,16].
Due to the presence of nanosized grains, nanocrystalline metals
generally suffer from grain coarsening when the temperature is elevated.
For extremely fine grains, grain growth occurs even at ambient temper-
ature. Previous experimental studies have shown that the grain coars-
ening temperature generally decreases with grain refinement [17]. Once
the grains of nanocrystalline metals grow, their strengths are signifi-
cantly reduced. Therefore, improving the thermal stability of nano-
crystalline metals and simultaneously ensuring high strength is of great
importance. Most recently, a Schwarz crystal with curved GBs con-
strained by coherent TB (CTB) networks was discovered in nanocrystal-
line Cu through experiments and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations
[18]. The curved GB resembles the Schwarz D-surface, which is a type of
triply periodic minimal surface. Nanocrystalline samples with a Schwarz
crystal structure (referred to as Schwarz nanocrystals) exhibited excellent

thermal stability. Schwarz nanocrystals with extremely fine grains
remained stable even when the temperature approached the melting–
point temperature
( T _ { \mathrm { m } } )
. Cu Schwarz nanocrystals with grain sizes of
nm exhibited a high strength of 1.57 GPa. Recent experimental studies
have shown that atomic diffusion in supersaturated Al Mg alloys is
effectively suppressed due to the presence of Schwarz crystals [19]. MD
simulations revealed that the excellent thermal stability of Schwarz
nanocrystals is attributed to the constraining effects of CTB networks and
that the minimum stable grain size is
{ \sim } 3 ~ \mathrm { n m }
Undoubtedly, the mechanical behaviors and properties of Schwarz
nanocrystals are closely associated with their grain size. To date, the
grain-size effect and associated deformation mechanisms of nano-
crystalline metals with Schwarz crystal structures remain unknown. In
this work, we performed a series of large-scale MD simulations via the
large-scale atomic/molecular massively parallel simulator (LAMMPS)
[21] to investigate the plastic deformation behaviors and grain-size effect
of nanocrystalline Cu with Schwarz crystal structure. During MD simu-–
lations, the interatomic interaction was described by an embedded atom
method potential [22], which can capture a variety of mechanical
properties and behaviors of Cu well [11 13,22] and has been recognized
as one of most accurate and reliable empirical potentials for Cu.
2. Methods
2.1. Construction and equilibration of simulated samples with Schwarz
crystal structure
To construct a simulated unit cell, we first set up the five single-
crystalline cubic blocks with side length of
\sqrt { 2 } d
(d, grain size) and spe-
cific orientations in Table S1, and then truncated these blocks according
to the plane equations of CTBs and the approximate equation of Schwarz
D-surface. Finally, we placed the truncated blocks into the corresponding
parts shown in Fig. S1 and obtained a typical unit cell. Fig. S1 shows
different views of atomistic configuration of unit cell of Schwarz crystal
we constructed. We further constructed the overall simulated sample by
periodically arranging the unit cell in form of
2 \times 2 \times 2
. The grain size
d of simulated Schwarz crystal varies from 2.33 nm to 15.41 nm. All the
simulated samples were equilibrated by energy minimization and sub-
sequent dynamics relaxation. During dynamics relaxation, all the sam-
ples were first relaxed for 500 ps at
3 0 0 \mathrm { K } ,
and then underwent a thermal
treatment (including heating to 600 K, holding for 500 ps at
6 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } ,
, and
cooling to 300 K), and were re-relaxed for 100 ps at 300 K. The rate of
temperature change during heating and cooling is
0 . 6 ~ \mathrm { K } / \mathrm { p s }
. During dy-
namics relaxation, the time step was set as 1 fs, and an isothermal-
isobaric (NPT) ensemble [23] was utilized to control the temperature
and to keep the pressure to be zero. Throughout MD simulations, periodic
boundary conditions were imposed in all three directions of simulated
samples.
2.2. Calculations for grain-boundary energy of simulated samples with
Schwarz crystal structure
The GB energies
\gamma _ { \mathtt { G B } }
of all the simulated Schwarz crystals with
different grain sizes at
_ { \mathrm { ~ 0 ~ K ~ } }
were calculated by using the following
expression,
\gamma_ {\mathrm {G B}} = \frac {E _ {\mathrm {t o t}} - N _ {\mathrm {F C C}} E _ {0} - A _ {\mathrm {C T B}} \gamma_ {\mathrm {C T B}}}{A _ {\mathrm {G B}}} \tag {1}
where
E _ { \mathrm { t o t } }
is the total potential energy of simulated sample,
N _ { \mathrm { F C C } }
is the
number of FCC atom,
is the cohesive energy ( 3.54 eV/atom) [22],
A _ { \mathrm { C T B } }
is the area of CTB,
A _ { \mathrm { G B } }
is the area of GB and
\gamma _ { \mathrm { T B } }
is the CTB energy
( 2 2 . 2 \mathrm { m J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } )
2.3. Atomistic simulations for thermal stability of Schwarz crystal
We performed MD simulations for heating of all the simulated
Schwarz crystal samples with different grain size. During heating, the
time step was set as 2.5 fs, and the temperature was controlled via an NPT
ensemble [23] to increase from 300 K to 1500 K with an interval of 1 K at
a very slow rate of
8 ~ \mathrm { K } / \mathrm { n s }
. Such precise temperature control is used to
capture the critical temperature for grain coarsening and/or melting.
2.4. Atomistic simulations for uniaxial tension of Schwarz crystal
During simulations, all the samples were stretched to
strain
along the tensile direction
z
-axis) at a constant strain rate of
During simulations, the time step was set as 1.0 fs, and the NPT ensemble
[23] was used to keep the temperature at
3 0 0 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }
and to control the
stresses along the directions normal to tensile direction to be zero.
2.5. Atomistic simulations for the creep of Schwarz crystal with curved GBs
and Wigner-Seitz crystal with regular straight GBs
To compare the GB diffusion between curved and straight GBs, we
constructed the simulated nanocrystalline samples with regular straight
GBs [24] by periodically arranging the Wigner-Seitz unit cell in form of 2
\times 2 \times 2
. The constructed nanocrystalline samples have varied grain size
d
of
6 . 9 3 \ \mathrm { n m }
and
8 . 5 6 \ \mathrm { n m }
. The grains in the Wigner-Seitz crystal have
random lattice orientations, but the misorientation between neighboring
grains is large enough to ensure nearly all the GBs to be high-angle GB.
We first equilibrated the Wigner-Seitz crystals via energy minimization
and subsequent dynamics relaxation at
3 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K }
for 200 ps. During relax-
ation, we used an NPT ensemble [23] to keep the temperature at 300 K
and to control the pressure to be zero. Fig. S3 shows the atomic config-
uration of typical Wigner-Seitz crystal with mean grain size of
6 . 9 3 \ \mathrm { n m }
To mimic the creep, we stretched all the simulated samples along z axis
under different constant temperatures (720 K, 840 K, 960 K and 1080 K)
and constant applied stresses (0 MPa, 120 MPa, 180 MPa, 240 MPa, 300
MPa, 360 MPa, 420 MPa, 480 MPa, 540 MPa, 600 MPa and 660 MPa).
Each creep simulation was run for 400 ps. Before each creep simulation,
the equilibrated samples were relaxed for 200 ps as the temperature was
controlled to increase from
3 0 0 ~ \mathrm { ~ K ~ }
to a target creep temperature.
Throughout MD simulations, periodic boundary conditions were
imposed in all three directions of simulated samples. During the creep
simulations, we used the following equation to calculate the diffusion
coefficient
of simulated sample at the elevated temperature,
D = \frac {\left\langle \left| \mathbf {r} (t) - \mathbf {r} (0) \right| ^ {2} \right\rangle}{6 t} \tag {2}
where r(t) and
are the position of atom at time t and initial time,
respectively, and the angle brackets represent the statistical average. For
three-dimension polycrystals, the diffusivity calculated by Eq. (2)
is
actually an effective diffusivity, which is a mixture of GB diffusivity and
lattice diffusivity. According to the classic Maxwell-Garnett equation,
recent studies have demonstrated that the GB diffusivity
D _ { \tt G B }
is approx-
imately proportional to the effective diffusivity
D
D \approx \frac {2 \delta}{d - \delta} D _ {\mathrm {G B}} \tag {3}
where
\delta
is the GB width and
d
is the mean grain size. Generally, the GB
width in atomic configuration of polycrystal is taken as
1 \ \mathrm { \ n m }
. We
calculated the GB diffusivity
D _ { \mathrm { G B } }
for Schwarz and Wigner-Seitz nano-
crystals by combining Eqs. (2) and (3).
Fig. S4 shows a comparison of our calculated GB diffusivities at
different temperatures with those from previous experiments [26] and
H. Xing et al.
Nano Materials Science 6 (2024) 320 328
MD simulations [24]. It is seen from Fig. S4 that for given temperature,
the GB diffusivities of nanocrystalline metals are higher than those of
coarse-grained counterparts. Enhanced GB diffusivities in nanocrystal-
line metals are associated with structural relaxation, grain growth and
local excess free volume [27]. The GB diffusivities from our calculations
on nanocrystals are in the high-temperature regime, are close to those
from previous MD simulations [24] and are comparable to those
extrapolated from the experimental measurements in the
low-temperature regime [26]. These comparisons indicate that our
calculated GB diffusivities are reliable and reasonable.

(from our MD simulations) into Eq. (4). In the

2.6. Construction, equilibration and tension of mixed samples with both
Schwarz and Wigner-Seitz crystals
In the experimental nanocrystalline metals with Schwarz crystals,
Schwarz nanocrystals only occupy a certain volume fraction of the entire
polycrystals. Indeed, nanocrystalline metals consist of a mixture of both
Schwarz and regular nanocrystals. Therefore, we first constructed five
typical samples by mixing the Schwarz nanocrystal with regular nano-
crystal (represented by Wigner-Seitz crystal), and then investigated their
mechanical properties, deformation mechanisms and thermal stabilities.
The mixed samples were first equilibrated by conjugate gradient method
and then relaxed at
3 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K }
for 200 ps using an NPT ensemble. The time
step was set as 1 fs. After relaxation, the samples with grain size of 4.67
nm maintained a mixture feature of Schwarz and Wigner-Seitz nano-
crystals. Throughout simulation, periodic conditions were imposed in all
three directions of mixed samples. Fig. S7 shows the relaxed atomic
configurations of pure Schwarz sample, Wigner-Seitz sample and five
mixed samples with grain size of
4 . 6 7 \ \mathrm { n m }
. The mixed samples have the
Schwarz-crystal volume fraction of
, and
Furthermore, to investigate the grain size effect of mixed samples with
different Schwarz-crystal volume fractions, we constructed the mixed
samples with different grain sizes of
3 . 0 4 \ \mathrm { n m }
and
6 . 9 3 \ \mathrm { n m }
. Notably,
mixed samples with grain size of
3 . 0 4 \ \mathrm { n m }
underwent grain coarsening
and GB annihilation during relaxation, indicating their thermodynamic
instability at
3 0 0 \mathrm { K }
. We further performed uniaxial tensile simulations for
mixed samples with grain size of 4.67 nm and 6.93 nm. All mixed samples
were stretched to
strain along the
\boldsymbol { \mathscr { z } }
-axis at a constant strain rate of 5
. The time step was set as 1 fs. The NPT ensemble was used to
maintain the temperature at
3 0 0 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }
and zero pressure in
x
and
y
directions.
3. Results
3.1. Construction of atomic configurations of Schwarz nanocrystal
As illustrated by Fig. 1 and S1, the equilibrium unit cell of poly-
crystalline metals with Schwarz crystal consists of 15 parts that are
separated by the CTBs or Schwarz D-surface. The approximate equation
of the Schwarz D-surface is expressed as [30].

\begin{array}{l} \sin \left(\frac {\pi x}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \sin \left(\frac {\pi y}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \sin \left(\frac {\pi z}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) + \sin \left(\frac {\pi x}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \cos \left(\frac {\pi y}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \cos \left(\frac {\pi z}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \\ + \cos \left(\frac {\pi x}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \sin \left(\frac {\pi y}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \cos \left(\frac {\pi z}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) + \cos \left(\frac {\pi x}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \cos \left(\frac {\pi y}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) \sin \left(\frac {\pi z}{\sqrt {2} d}\right) = 0 \tag {5} \\ \end{array}
where
x , y ,
and
\boldsymbol { \mathscr { z } }
are the coordinates and
d
is the aperture of the Schwarz
D-surface (Fig. 1) and represents the grain size of the Schwarz crystal.
These separate parts have five different lattice orientations, which are
listed in Table S1. The curved GBs in the Schwarz crystal are generated
due to the misorientations between neighboring grains with different
twin lattice orientations (i.e., T1, T2, T3, and
\mathrm { T } _ { 4 }
listed in Table S1). This
implies that the curved GBs are constrained by CTB networks. We first
constructed the unit cell of Schwarz crystal and the simulated poly-
crystals with
2 \times 2 \times 2
unit cells and various grain sizes. Fig. 1 shows the
atomic configurations of the simulated Schwarz crystal with
d = 4 . 6 7 \ : \mathrm { n m }
after equilibration and its unit cell, GB structure and CTB networks. The
curved GB structure shown in Fig. 1d resembles the Schwarz D-surface
described by Eq. (5). The atomic configuration, GB structure and CTB
networks of the unit cell of our Schwarz crystal are nearly the same as
those of the Schwarz crystal transformed from extended Kelvin poly-
crystals from recent MD simulations [18] using different interatomic
potential. It is noted that our simulated Schwarz crystals are constructed
by referring the geometrical features of Schwarz nanocrystals reported in
recent pioneering MD simulations [18] and by using the approximate
equation (i.e. Eq. (5)) of the Schwarz D-surface to truncate some grains.
Such construction process is different from the evolution from the
extended Kelvin nanocrystals used in recent MD simulations [18]. The
extended
Kelvin
nanocrystals
consist
of
some
truncated-octahedral-shaped grains with the same size and low-energy
grain facets [18]. During evolution, the {111} grain facets (i.e. CTBs)
remain stable against thermal fluctuation, while other grain facets
participate in the formation of curved GB-network of Schwarz crystals
[18]. In recent MD simulations, the entire evolution from the extended
Kelvin nanocrystals to Schwarz nanocrystals generally took
ns
[18]. However, the equilibration of initial samples in our MD simulations
took hundreds of picoseconds, since our initial atomic configurations are
closer to the equilibrium states, compared with the Kelvin nanocrystals
used in previous MD simulations [18]. Using our construction approach,
we can easily generate the simulated Schwarz samples with different
grain sizes and further investigate the grain size effect in Schwarz
nanocrystals. During MD simulations, we adopted common neighbor
analysis [31] to identify the local crystalline order of atoms and defects
during deformation. Atoms with face-centered-cubic (FCC) order are
painted in green, atoms with hexagonal-close-packed (HCP) order are
painted in red, and the disordered atoms are painted in gray.–

energies γ of all the simulated Schwarz crystals at 0 K. The calculated
GB energies are in the range of
, which is close to that
from recent MD simulations [18]. Notably, the GB
energy is independent of the grain size (Fig. S2). We also calculated
elastic constants
, Poisson's ratio and the bulk modulus) at 0 K
and the associated Zener ratios
\begin{array} { r } { ( A _ { z } = \frac { 2 c _ { 4 4 } } { c _ { 1 1 } - c _ { 2 2 } } ) } \end{array}
2c44 ) of the simulated Schwarz
crystal unit cell with

. Table S2 compares the calculated

close to those from previous MD simulations [18].

3.2. Thermal stability of Schwarz nanocrystal
Recent experimental studies and MD simulations [18,20] investigated
the thermal stability of nanocrystalline Cu with Schwarz crystal and
showed that a Schwarz crystal with minimum grain size up to
{ \sim } 3 ~ \mathrm { n m }
remains stable even at a temperature close to the melting point
T _ { \mathrm { m } }
K) of Cu. Note that the interatomic potential used in our MD simulations
is different from that used in previous MD simulations [18,20]. Here, we
further validate the dependence of the thermal stability of the Schwarz
crystal on the grain size by performing MD simulations based on the
interatomic potential [22] and our constructed sample. Fig. 2a shows the
variation in potential energy per atom with temperature during heating
of all simulated samples. The potential energy of all the simulated sam-
ples suddenly jumps at a specific temperature during heating. Such a
jump means that the overall Schwarz crystal structure becomes thermally
unstable due to grain coarsening or melting (i.e., transforming into an
amorphous state). The temperature at the energy jump is taken as the
critical temperature
( T _ { \mathrm { c } } )
, which characterizes the thermal stability of
materials. Fig. 2b shows the critical temperature as a function of the grain
size of samples from our MD simulations and previous experiments and
MD simulations [18,20]. As shown in Fig. 2b, our simulation results are
in good agreement with those from previous experimental measurements
and MD simulations [18,20]. The minimum grain size of the stable
Schwarz crystal near
T _ { \mathrm { m } }
is as large as 3.82 nm. The critical temperatures
T _ { \mathrm { c } }
of simulated samples with
and
3 . 0 4 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
are lower than the
melting point. In these samples, the fraction of FCC atoms sharply in-
creases at
T _ { \mathrm { c } } ,
while the fractions of atoms on both GB and CTB suddenly
decrease at
T _ { \mathrm { c } } ,
as exemplified by Fig. 2c. It implies that the grain coars-
ening occurs in these samples. Such grain coarsening is associated with
the migration or vanishing of GBs and CTBs, as evidenced by the insets in
Fig. 2c. When the grain size is up to
3 . 8 2 \ \mathrm { n m }
and even larger, the
T _ { \mathrm { c } }
is
close to the
T _ { \mathrm { m } }
. In the simulated samples with
d \geq 3 . 8 2  { \mathrm { n m } }
, the fraction of
GB atoms suddenly increases to nearly
at
T _ { \mathrm { c } } ,
while both FCC and
CTB atoms almost disappear completely (Fig. 2d). It implies that the
Schwarz crystal structure transforms into the amorphous structure (insets
in Fig. 2d) at
T _ { \mathrm { c } } ,
i.e. the crystal structure melts with the collapse of GBs
and CTBs. It is noted that for the simulated samples with
d \geq 3 . 8 2  { \mathrm { n m } }
, the
CTBs always remain stable before and even near melting and have a
certain constraints on the curved GBs [18], so that no grain coarsening
occurs before melting. It suggests that excellent thermal stability of
Schwarz nanocrystals is associated with the stable CTBs and their con-
straints on the curved GBs.
By combining two kinds of nanocrystals shown in Figs. S7a and b, we
constructed the mixed samples containing both Schwarz and Wigner-
Seitz nanocrystals with grain size of
4 . 6 7 \ \mathrm { n m }
and
6 . 9 3 \ \mathrm { n m }
(Fig. S8a).
We further investigated the thermal stabilities of these mixed sample by
heating them and compared them with pure Wigner-Seitz or Schwarz
samples. Periodic conditions were imposed in all three directions of
simulated sample. The time step was set as 2.5 fs. The temperature was
increased from
3 0 0 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }
at a very slow rate of
8 ~ \mathrm { K } / \mathrm { n s }
using an NPT
ensemble. Fig. S8b shows the variation in potential energy per atom of
simulated samples with temperature during heating. At a critical tem-
perature (i.e.
T _ { \mathrm { c } } )
, the simulated sample experienced a significant change–
in potential energy, indicating the onset of its melting or instability.
Notably, the critical temperature of mixed sample is lower than pure
Wigner-Seitz or Schwarz samples with the same grain size. Figs. S8c 8e
presents a sequence of snapshots of mixed sample with grain size of 6.93–
nm at different temperatures. Around the critical temperature of 788 K,
GBs in Wigner-Seitz parts start to migrate, leading to the subsequent–
grain coarsening (indicated by black arrows in Figs. S8d 8e). The CTB–
networks in Schwarz parts gradually collapse with the diminishing of
CTBs (indicated by dashed lines in Figs. S8d 8e), leading to further
annihilation of GBs in Schwarz parts. Figs. S8f 8h present a sequence of
snapshots of pure Wigner-Seitz sample. Around the critical temperature
of 914 K, GB migration leads to grain coarsening. Thus, the mixed sample
has a worse thermal stability than pure Schwarz or Wigner-Seitz nano-
crystals, due to the existence of high-energy interface between Schwarz
and Wigner-Seitz parts.
3.3. Deformation behaviors and grain-size effect
To investigate the deformation behaviors and grain-size effects of

During plastic deformation, all the curved GBs remain nearly intact, and
most of the pre-existing CTBs are still complete. This implies the intrinsic
stability of CTBs during plastic deformation [33] and their constraints on–
the curved GBs. A large number of Shockley partial dislocations nucleate
and then slip on different slip planes and interact with each other–
(Fig. 4j l). Undoubtedly, both the GBs and CTB networks block the motion
of these dislocations, contributing to the strengthening. The simulation
results in Fig. 4a l indicate that for nanocrystalline samples with Schwarz
crystals, when
d < d _ { \mathrm { c } } ,
the interfacial activities (including CTB migration
and annihilation of GBs and CTBs) dominate the plastic deformation;
otherwise, the nucleation and slip of dislocations is a controlling defor-
mation mechanism. More details about the deformation of two represen-

Fig.
4 \mathrm { m } { - } 0
captures a detailed process of annihilation of GBs and CTBs
during plastic deformation of a Schwarz crystal with
d < d _ { \mathrm { c } }
. During
deformation, partial dislocations nucleate from the intersections between
CTBs and curved GBs and then glide on the CTBs, leading to CTB
migration and further thinning of twin domains (i.e., detwinning).
Notably, curved GBs are generated due to misorientations between
neighboring grains with different twin lattice orientations in Table S1.
Once the twin domains on both sides of the curved GBs lose the twin
orientations and convert to the matrix orientations with detwinning, the
curved GBs annihilate spontaneously (Fig. 4o). As the CTBs disappear,
the curved GBs constrained by these CTBs vanish gradually. This process
prevails in all Schwarz crystals with
d < d _ { \mathrm { c } }
. Previous studies on the plastic
deformation of nanotwinned metals indicated that dislocation nucleation
from the CTB-GB intersection and subsequent detwinning induce a sig-
nificant decrease in strength with a reduction in twin thickness [8,13].
Similarly, for Schwarz crystals with curved GBs and CTB networks,
dislocation nucleation from the CTB-GB intersection and subsequent
detwinning inevitably contribute to softening. Simultaneously, as the
curved GBs and CTB networks gradually annihilate, the dislocation mo-
tion within the grain interior is not blocked by GBs and CTBs, leading to
further softening. Therefore, the softening in Schwarz crystal with
d < d _ { \mathrm { c } }
is attributed to dislocation nucleation from CTB-GB intersection, subse-
quent detwinning (or CTB migration) and further annihilation of GBs and
CTBs.

4. Discussion
Our MD simulations revealed that the strengthening mechanism in
the Schwarz crystal is the GBs blocking dislocation motion, while the
softening is mainly attributed to CTB migration and annihilation of GBs
and CTBs. Notably, the strengthening mechanism in the Schwarz crystal
is the same as that in nanocrystalline metals with regular GBs, but the
softening mechanism is distinct from that (i.e., GB-mediated processes,– –
including GB sliding, diffusion and migration) in nanocrystalline metals
with regular GBs. As indicated by Fig. 3b, the critical grain size–
) in Schwarz crystal is smaller than that
) in
nanocrystalline samples with regular GBs. For Schwarz crystals with
smaller grain sizes (i.e.,
d < 1 0 { - } 1 5 \ \mathrm { n m }
), the volume fraction of GBs

nanocrystals (Fig. 5a). Subsequently, we further investigated the GB-
mediated processes in Schwarz crystals and the influence of grain size
on these processes. For comparison, we also constructed Wigner Seitz
nanocrystals with regular straight GBs, which is a typical polycrystalline–
configuration and generally used to investigate GB behaviors [24]. We
performed MD simulations of the uniaxial tension and creep of Schwarz
and Wigner Seitz nanocrystals to compare the influences of GB struc-
tures on GB-mediated processes in these two kinds of nanocrystals.
Generally, atomic-level deformation can be decomposed into affine
plastic deformation and nonaffine atomic rearrangement [32]. The affine
deformation involves ordered transformation and is related to dislocation
slip and twinning processes, while the nonaffine deformation is related to
the activities of amorphous-like GBs, such as GB migration and sliding.
The nonaffine deformation is described by the nonaffine squared
displacement
D _ {i} ^ {2} = \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n _ {i}} \left| \mathbf {d} _ {i j} - \mathbf {J} _ {i} \mathbf {d} _ {i j} ^ {0} \right| ^ {2} \tag {6}
where
and
represent the distance vector between atoms i and
j
in
the undeformed and current states, respectively,
n _ { i }
is the total number of
neighboring atoms around atom i, and
{ \bf J } _ { i }
is the affine deformation tensor
of atom i and is determined by minimizing
. The average minimum of–
over all the atoms
( < D _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ^ { 2 } > )
) can be used to measure the extent of GB
migration and sliding. Fig. 5b shows the variations in the average non-–
affine squared displacement
in Schwarz and Wigner Seitz
nanocrystals with different grain sizes under tension. For the given grain–
size, the Schwarz nanocrystal has a lower
than the Wigner Seitz
nanocrystal throughout tension (Fig. 5b). This result suggests that
compared with the Wigner Seitz nanocrystal, GB migration and sliding
are suppressed in the Schwarz nanocrystal, especially around the critical
grain size. This can be explained by the fact that the volume fraction of
GBs is relatively low in Schwarz nanocrystals (Fig. 5a). Moreover, the
curved GBs are constrained by CTB networks. If the curved GBs migrate
or slide, coordinated deformation of the CTB networks is required. Thus,
the constraint of CTB networks to some extent restricts the migration and–
sliding of curved GBs.

5. Conclusions
In summary, we performed large-scale MD simulations to investigate
the GB structure, thermal stability, deformation behaviors and grain-size
effect of nanocrystalline Cu with Schwarz crystal structure. The results
regarding the GB structure and thermal stability from our MD simulations
agree well with those from recent experimental studies and MD simula-
tions [18,20]. Our MD simulations further showed that the critical grain
size for the strengthening-softening transition in Schwarz nanocrystals is
smaller than that in regular nanocrystals, leading to higher strength than
regular nanocrystals. The decrease in the critical grain size is associated
with the suppression of the GB-mediated processes in Schwarz nano-
crystals. Our simulations also revealed that softening in Schwarz nano-
crystals is attributed to detwinning (or CTB migration) and annihilation
of GBs and CTBs instead of the dominant softening mechanism (i.e.,
GB-mediated processes) in regular nanocrystals. Our current study pro-
vides a fundamental understanding of the plastic deformation and
grain-size effect of nanocrystalline metals with Schwarz crystal structure,
which guides the design and fabrication of new nanostructured metals
with high strength and excellent thermal stability.
